removed "North Korea" from the "nominally Communist government" list. reason : North Korea is not Marxist-Leninist or Communist, they officially removed Marxism-Leninism from their constitution in 1992 and replaced it with Juche, Songun and loyalty to the Kim bloodline (though Juche was unofficially dominating over Marxism-Leninism from the 1970s itself, but official adoption happened in the year 1992). By 2009, all references to communism were removed. North Korea is hence NOT a communist govt.

Undid revision [[Special:Diff/1291466479|1291466479]] by [[Special:Contributions/ApricotAvalanche037|ApricotAvalanche037]] ([[User talk:ApricotAvalanche037|talk]]) revert edits based on erroneous opinion

It is a misnomer that Marxism claims that communism will be stateless (abolition of the codified social organisation of society). "Statelessness" in Marxism refers to no longer needing a heightened force of the state to suppress bourgeois reaction as it has been eliminated as a class. Much like how the development of liberalism involved Robespierre, but now it has solidified itself politically without needing the same level of force, until it runs into its own contradictions.
"Communist party-ruled" instead of "Communist party-ruling" (grammar). These footnotes in general could use some attention as the grammar is sometimes difficult to parse.

My edit aimed to fix problems like this, and to improve some awkward wording (e.g. "argue" instead of "posit"). Nevertheless, here is a more limited tweak.

Deleted words not backed by the cited source (a tertiary source) after reading the discussion on the topic in the talk page. The wikilink to "left-wing" defines "left-wing" as "a range" of political ideologies. It makes little sense to define another range as something that goes from a previously defined range (which includes center-left) to a position in the spectrum that is also included in the wikilinked left-wing range.

One of Wikipedia’s most ridiculous articles ever. Classifying communism as "left-wing" is completely ignorant to the entirety of the political spectrum; communism far-left, socialism left-wing to far-left, liberalism centre-left, libertarianism centre, conservatism centre-right, nationalism right-wing, and fascism far-right. Communism is far left and trying to define it as anything else is impossible. Reverting the far-left title should never have occurred.

v2.04 - Repaired 1 link to disambiguation page - [[WP:DPL|(You can help)]] - [[Holocaust (disambiguation)]] / Fix errors for [[WP:WCW|CW project]] (Reference duplication - Template with deprecated parameter)
/* Dissolution of the Soviet Union */ restoring citation as material came from this source

/* Dissolution of the Soviet Union */ these comments on saving the "democratic project" from the "the death grip of neoliberalism" have nothing to do with the subject this section is supposed to present, e.g.: "History-> Dissolution of the Soviet Union". Let's stay on topic!
/* Dissolution of the Soviet Union */ making attribution clearer; the criticism comes from Dean and her 2012 book The Communist Horizon
/* Excess deaths under Communist states */ too long sentence. Rephrasing a bit.
/* Cold War */ further info on the Cold War, link to the main Wikipedia article
/* Cold War */ improper start of sentence

/* Post-Soviet Communism */ scholars are not "outside commentators". Besides, the opinions of "outside commentators" should not be quoted on Wikipedia. This is not Twitter! Some claims in the paragraph should be backed by citations, too

/* Cold War */ most scholars describe China as a Communist state, not as "nationalistic state capitalism" (this view is supported by few scholars). Also: "scholars" are "experts" on Communism, no need to present two categories of critics here, implying one of them (scholars) are not the other (experts).

/* Reception */ unsourced claim / synthesis removed. Also, the "double genocide" theory is already mentioned 5-6 times in the article, including in the following sub-sections of the "analysis". This whole "Analysis" section is a huge mess.

/* Cold War */ this whole sentence/paragraph reads like original research, is poorly documented (with major claims not backed by citations) and seems to ignore the more complex perspective offered by the better documented articles on wikipedia on the Nazi vs Soviet topics

/* Cold War */ a search on Google Scholar shows that post 2009 there are more than 42000 books and academic papers referencing "totalitarianism". The concept might be criticized by some academics, but data shows is definitely not "defunct" as this sentence implies. The "defunct" claim also quotes a fringe article with a very small number of citations

/* Excess deaths under Communist states */ claims that call into question or slander major documents (like the Prague Declaration), which have wide support including among historians, should be better documented, and (especially) have attribution.

/* Memory and legacy */ the "Victims of Communism" concept was not named after the "Victims of Communism Memorial Foundation". The opposite seems to be the case.  The "Victims of Communism" concept is used in scholarly papers published between 1950-1990, before the foundation was even created.
/* Memory and legacy */ Reintroducing other change lost in revert
Reverting order. Starting with "victims of communism" is not neutral.
/* Memory and legacy */ according to his wikipedia page Courtois is a historian
/* Memory and legacy */  the wikipedia article for the book being mentioned explains really well what the books is all about. No need for non-neutral hype cherry-picked from obscure blogs.
/* Memory and legacy */ moved up paragraphs clarifying what "memory and legacy" is all about
/* Memory and legacy */ Davide Mastracci is a blogger, not a scholar, and not relevant or reliable source for this topic
/* Memory and legacy */ concepts used by large masses of historians are not mere narratives
